metadata.title:
COMPERS: THE KIND OF man WHO


LEADS TWO MILLION WORKERS

BY gibson GARDNER

pages.raw_text:
WASHINGTON, Dec. 20.-The
voice of 100000 working men
the embodiment of union labor in
America-Samuel Gompers, presi-
dent of the American Federation of
Labor, Just reelected to that posi-
ton for the Gath annual term
what manner of man is he?


We were having a little informal
talk and worldly riches were men
toned. Bribery in high and low
places soon drifted into the discus
sion. Mr.. Gompers said nobody had
ever tried to bride him. Then he
remarked:


81 believe there is less bribery
than is commonly supposed. On
the whole, men are pretty honest.
That is my experience. If they are
not, they are quickly found out and
sent 10 the rear


Are you rich, Mr.. Gompers?'


The president of the American
Federation seemed surprised, then
amused.


DO you own any real estate?"


Not a shovelful of sand


"Any stocks, bonds or securities
anything from which you may
clip coupons?"


Not a share of anything. have
not an investment. | have no es-
tate, and when ! die all | will leave
my family will be $3.50, my Cigar
makers union insurance.


81 have no other property.
have no ambition to have any. |
do not care for money. | believe
can get the few things that l
want-if not as president of the
American Federation, at my trade
or some other work. | am poor
l am proud to say that | am poor.


But you have had chances to
make money!


Il have. Offers and opportunities
have come to MEETINGS which
would have made me money. | was
interested in other things


And you feel contented with
your "a years of service as organ
izer and manager of the American
Federation?'


81 shall feel contented to die with
no estate if l can go with the feel
ing that l have done something to
serve my fellowmen, my fellow
workmen.' ..- ..-.--r


"And you feel that you have in
fact been of service to them?"


Il most certainly do. Their con-
ditions are better than they were.
1t is hard to understand how much
better the condition of the work
man is now than it was 20 or 30
years ago. The man of small in
come has more now than he used
to. and he has more time to enjoy
it. He has more of the comforts
of life"


(Which is due to organization ?"


yes"


"And you feel that the Fedora
ton is sufficient monument and
legacy?"


Il am proud of the American
Federation. | little dreamed when
I was first associated with the
work, what it would come to. The
first six years l served as presi-
dent without pay. | worked and
supported my family at my trade
as cigarmaker. Evenings and holi-
days and half days Off | devoted to
work of organization.


cover in New York We had one
little room in tenement--one
room and my bedroom. That was
the Federation's headquarters. Now
see. We are In this fine building.
We have all this floor and several
rooms in the basement. We have
40 clerks and 1850 organizers. Our
membership is in the neighborhood
of 100000. We have a record Of
accom- plishment; better wages, bet
ter industrial conditions, some good
